result:
I’m going to take your request quite literally: 2500 words, free topic, but not just filler. I’ll weave together a single long essay about a theme that quietly threads through technology, art, and everyday life: how we choose what to pay attention to.

---

## The Quiet Tyranny of the Interesting

Every minute, more things are trying to capture your attention than at any point in human history.

Your phone lights up with a notification. Three apps silently push updated feeds. The browser tab shows a red bubble in the corner. A video auto-plays. Somewhere, a friend posts a photo. Somewhere else, an algorithm calculates the next thing most likely to keep you from closing the app.

You already know this, abstractly. But what’s less obvious is something stranger: we’ve started to internalize the logic of these systems, and we apply it to ourselves. We’re starting to believe that everything in life has to be “interesting” in the same way that a video, a tweet, or a headline is interesting.

Boring things now feel almost morally suspect. Long, slow efforts without constant reward feel wrong, like we’re doing something inefficient or outdated. We judge activities by how “engaging” they are. We even judge people this way.

This is the quiet tyranny of the interesting.

What gets lost under that tyranny isn’t just peace or quiet—what we lose is the ability to notice what actually matters to us, instead of what is best at pleasing the machinery of attention.

---

## What It Means to Notice

“Pay attention” sounds like a moral command, but grammatically it’s a financial metaphor. You *pay* attention, as if you own some limited resource and you decide where to spend it.

Most of us treat attention like small coins we scatter on the ground whenever something makes a sound. The economy is reversed: instead of carefully investing it, we basically stand in a crowd and throw our wallet at whoever yells loudest.

Slowness is essential for noticing. Not in the romanticized sense of a cabin in the woods, but in the more prosaic sense of letting a single thing occupy the foreground of your experience for longer than a moment, without splitting it into parallel tabs. The frictionless world we’re building hates this. Scrolling is the opposite of slowness. Swiping, too. They’re finely tuned to favor novelty over depth.

The tragedy is that some of the most meaningful parts of life show up in forms that are initially indistinguishable from boredom.

- The first 20 minutes of a walk where nothing special happens.
- The early phase of reading a dense book, before understanding kicks in.
- The first days of learning a skill, when it mostly feels like incompetence.
- The silent part of a conversation where nobody quite knows what to say yet.

These are liminal zones where old habits don’t yet pay off and new patterns haven’t formed. They’re rarely “interesting” in a quick-hit sense. But they are fertile.

When you endure those zones without reflexively reaching for more stimulation, you start to notice things you’d never see otherwise: small distinctions, half-formed thoughts, subtle emotions, connections between seemingly unrelated ideas. That noticing is the raw material out of which almost all creativity, self-knowledge, and insight are built.

---

## The Algorithm in Your Head

Out in the tech world, recommendation systems and ranking algorithms are optimized for a blend of metrics: time on site, clicks, shares, watch time, and so on. To do this, they learn from your immediate reactions: Did you tap? Did you linger? Did you share?

You have something similar inside your mind: a mental “algorithm” that ranks experiences by how rewarding they feel. But your brain doesn’t use watch time and click-through. It uses quick neurological signals: pleasure, relief from discomfort, novelty, social approval.

Nothing about this is new; human brains have always worked this way. What *is* new is the environment in which these internal algorithms now operate. They used to be calibrated in a world where boredom was abundant and stimulation was scarce. Failing to chase curiosity or novelty meant possibly missing out on learning an important skill or social development.

Now the polarity is reversed: stimulation is abundant, boredom is scarce. But the brain’s wiring hasn’t updated. The same impulses that once nudged you toward valuable learning now can be exploited by systems tuned to maximize engagement, not long-term meaning.

Gradually, without ever consciously deciding to, you start to internalize external metrics of value:

- “Did this get likes?” becomes “Was this worth doing?”
- “Did I finish a season?” becomes “Was this a good use of time?”
- “Did people respond quickly?” becomes “Was it okay to say that?”

The external metrics seep inward. Eventually, your inner judge of value begins to resemble a social media feed ranking model: things that get immediate reaction and pleasure rise to the top, while slow-burn, long-term, or quiet endeavors appear low-value and fade out of your personal awareness.

This is how people end up saying things like, “I used to paint, but I don’t anymore,” or “I used to read books, but I can’t focus now,” without realizing that nothing about them became “less creative” or “less disciplined.” They just started using a different ranking function to decide what deserved attention.

Their internal algorithm was updated—without their consent.

---

## The Discomfort of Doing One Thing

Deep work, focused reading, meaningful conversations, making art, real learning—they all share an unglamorous trait: they are often uncomfortable at the beginning.

The discomfort has many flavors:

- **Cognitive strain:** It takes effort to comprehend new concepts or sustain a chain of reasoning.
- **Emotional vulnerability:** It can feel exposing to make something or say something that might be judged.
- **Ego threat:** Struggling at a new skill contradicts the identity of “competent person.”
- **Restlessness:** Your brain, used to rapid novelty, protests when things slow down.

If you’ve been immersed for years in high-stimulation environments—constant tabs, notifications, short-form content—this discomfort feels sharper. Doing one thing at a time doesn’t just feel “less fun”; it feels almost wrong, like diet food when you’re used to intense sweetness.

This is where many people misdiagnose the problem. They conclude:

- “I guess I’m just not a reader.”
- “I must have a short attention span.”
- “I’m too old to learn new things.”

More often, what’s happening is: your nervous system has been trained on a different baseline. You’re trying to run long-distance after habitually sprinting between tiny bursts of novelty. The discomfort is withdrawal more than incapacity.

If you can sit with that discomfort instead of automatically medicating it with more stimulation, a peculiar thing happens: a second mode of attention comes online.

At first, the mind chatters: I should check my phone; this is boring; why is this taking so long? If you don’t obey those impulses, the chatter eventually gets tired. Beneath it, a quieter stream of thought appears, one that wasn’t audible before because it had no space to emerge.

This quieter mode is where the more complex, layered, non-obvious thoughts live. It’s where your actual opinions, doubts, questions, and subtle intuitions dwell—the parts that can’t be compressed into a sound bite. These aren’t necessarily profound in a lofty sense, but they tend to be more honest, more original, and more “yours” than the reflexive reactions you have during constant stimulation.

This mode doesn’t scream for attention. You have to stay put long enough to meet it.

---

## Depth vs. Width

The internet is very good at width: more sources, more views, more information, more voices, more options. This is extraordinary. You can learn the basics of almost anything in days that used to take years of apprenticeship.

But your lived life is still constrained by an older physics. You don’t get infinite time or energy. You can’t live 50 lives; you get roughly one. Choosing depth in any direction inevitably means narrowing width.

Depth is not always glamorous. Imagine two people learning guitar:

- Person A watches dozens of short videos about tips, gear reviews, and advanced tricks, hopping around, inspired and excited but playing only a few minutes here and there.
- Person B chooses one simple chord progression and repeats it badly, for an hour, every evening, for weeks.

Person A is “more engaged.” They might know more about the instrument. Person B, from a metrics standpoint, is boring: repetitive, low variety, not shareable. But months later, Person B can sit down and play something recognizable. They’ve paid the tax of boredom and acquired embodied skill.

The same pattern holds in learning languages, writing, programming, drawing, relationships, even understanding yourself. You either endure the dull repetitions, or you skate forever on the shiny surface.

The frightening thing is that our tools increasingly push us toward width. Autoplay gives us more videos; endless scroll gives us more posts; recommendation feeds give us more sources. Meanwhile, the quiet, repetitive engagement that produces skill and understanding looks, on a screen, almost indistinguishable from doing nothing.

Nobody claps for you while you revise a paragraph five times, or practice the same piano measure, or reread a complex passage. There is no engagement metric for sitting in silence with a feeling until it makes sense.

Yet these are the exact activities that shape who you become.

---

## Boredom as a Compass

Boredom has a terrible reputation. It’s treated as a failure mode, a sign that something is wrong. With so much entertainment and information available, being bored feels outdated, like losing Wi-Fi in a modern city.

But boredom—left unsedated—acts as a kind of compass. It points in two directions at once:

1. **Away from misalignment.** Persistent boredom in a job, relationship, or project can signal that your values and circumstances are out of sync. Sometimes, what you’re doing simply does not matter to you in any deep way.
2. **Toward latent curiosity.** When you don’t automatically reach for your phone, boredom begins to morph into small, tentative questions: What is actually going on with me? What did I use to care about? What am I putting off thinking about? Those questions are a doorway.

The trouble is, we rarely give boredom enough unsedated time to transform. We kill it as soon as it flickers. Standing in a line, we check our phone. Waiting for someone, we scroll. Sitting alone, we turn on background noise.

The message to yourself is clear: any gap in stimulation is unacceptable. But those gaps are where self-generated interests appear. A life where every moment is filled with external input gradually starves the internal generator of topics and questions. You become highly entertained and vaguely empty.

Letting yourself be bored—really bored—for a while is uncomfortable because it confronts you with the fact that a lot of your mental life is downstream of what other people decide to show you. That realization can be unsettling, but it’s also empowering. If your attention can be captured, it can also be reclaimed.

---

## The Myth of Optimization

Modern culture loves the idea of optimization: hack your workflow, maximize productivity, squeeze more output out of less time. The underlying belief is that our main problem is inefficiency: we are not getting enough done.

Sometimes that’s true, but just as often the opposite is: we’re doing too many things too quickly, and almost none of them are the ones that matter most.

People search for the best note-taking system, the perfect productivity app, the ideal time-blocking method. These tools can help, but they share an assumption: that you already know what deserves your focus. They don’t help you decide what’s actually worth doing—they help you do, faster, whatever you’ve already chosen.

If your choices are misaligned—if you’re optimizing for other people’s metrics (status, appearances, easy approval)—then optimization only deepens the problem. You become extremely efficient at climbing a ladder that’s leaning against the wrong wall.

A more unsettling, and more useful, question than “How can I be more productive?” is “What would I still do if nobody ever knew I did it?”

That question bypasses a lot of the social and algorithmic ranking systems. It forces you to confront which activities are intrinsically meaningful to you, rather than just instrumentally valuable for some external payoff.

Examples vary:

- Writing, even if nobody reads it.
- Practicing music, even if you never perform.
- Learning a subject, even if it doesn’t advance your career.
- Walking with no step counter or fitness metric.
- Calling a friend just to hear how they are, not to get or give advice.

These rarely look “optimal.” They won’t necessarily scale. They may not impress. But they form the bedrock of a life led from the inside out.

---

## Friction as a Feature

Much of the design ethos around technology has been to remove friction. One-click buying, auto-play, default logins, push notifications: all meant to minimize the effort between wanting something and getting it.

For some tasks this is undeniably good—paying bills, accessing information quickly in emergencies, coordinating logistics. But when everything becomes effortless, we lose the natural pauses that used to serve as checkpoints for reflection.

Years ago, if you wanted a new book, you had to go to a store or library. The travel, the browsing, the walking home—all of that downtime gave your mind chances to reconsider: Do I really want this? What am I actually looking for?

Now, you can tap twice and have a book on your device in seconds. That’s convenient, but the decision is compressed to a momentary impulse, easily swayed by a polished description or a spike of curiosity that might vanish an hour later.

Friction, in moderation, protects you from your own whims.

There’s a strong case for intentionally reintroducing a little friction into your own attention economy:

- Deleting apps that are too easily opened; using them only in a browser.
- Turning off non-essential notifications entirely.
- Keeping certain activities (like reading or writing) tied to physical locations or objects.
- Adding small rituals before starting something important: a cup of tea, a walk, a few deep breaths.

These aren’t about becoming austere or Luddite. They’re about giving your reflective mind a fighting chance to step in before your reflexive habits steer everything.

When there’s a brief delay between impulse and action, you get a tiny window in which to ask: Is this really how I want to spend the next 30 minutes of my one life?

---

## The Value of Doing Things Badly

Another subtle effect of the attention economy is a distorted sense of competence. Online, you mostly encounter highly polished results: the best performances, the wittiest comments, the most aesthetic photos, the most articulate essays. The learning curves are hidden.

When you try something new and your first attempts are clumsy, it’s easy to feel outclassed, as if you’re miles behind some invisible standard. Combined with a feed that’s optimized to show you the most impressive examples, this can be paralyzing.

This is where the skill of *doing things badly* becomes crucial.

Doing something badly—not carelessly, but as a beginner—requires a quiet defiance. You’re saying, in effect:

“I am willing to be unimpressive for as long as it takes to build real competence.”

That stance is almost the opposite of what most algorithmic environments reward. Those environments favor *rapid micro-impressions*—a joke, a take, a highlight reel—that can be evaluated in seconds. Slow, uneven, incremental progression can’t compete in that space.

But away from the screen, in real human time, the willingness to be bad is the gatekeeper for growth. If you protect your image too carefully, if you’re unwilling to show up as a beginner, you shrink your life to only those arenas where you can already perform.

Some of the richest experiences you can have—learning an instrument, speaking a new language with real people, trying a new physical activity, making art, having vulnerable conversations—require a long period of being not-very-good. Online, that’s embarrassing. Offline, it’s just called “learning.”

If you can detach your sense of worth from immediate excellence, you free yourself to explore much more territory. You become less dependent on external validation and more guided by curiosity and inherent satisfaction.

---

## Attention as an Ethical Act

We usually talk about attention as a private matter: my focus, my habits, my productivity. But where you place your attention also has ethical dimensions, because attention is part of how value circulates in a culture.

Every time you click, share, watch, buy, or respond, you send a bit of signal into a complex network of systems. That signal—aggregated with millions of others—affects what gets made, who gets paid, what people think is acceptable, and what spreads.

This doesn’t mean your individual actions control the world. But they participate in shaping it.

- Giving attention to thoughtful, nuanced work—articles, books, conversations—supports an environment where nuance can survive.
- Feeding outrage cycles, even when you’re “hate-watching” or doomscrolling, strengthens the patterns that produce more of the same.
- Choosing to listen deeply to a friend instead of half-listening while scrolling sends a signal in the opposite direction: that depth and presence matter more than ambient distraction.

In this sense, attention functions a bit like money or votes. You don’t control the whole system, but you do participate. Small, repeated choices compound.

This doesn’t require becoming austere or moralistic about everything you consume. It does suggest, though, that you’re never entirely a passive observer. You are, in ways both subtle and direct, a contributor to the environment you inhabit.

Attending to something is not only a way of seeing; it’s also a way of saying, quietly: “Let there be more of this.”

---

## Reclaiming a Piece of Your Mind

If all of this sounds abstract, here’s a simple way to make it concrete. Suppose you wanted, very modestly, to reclaim some of your attention from the default currents around you. You might try something like this:

1. **Set aside one “notification-free” block per day.**  
   Not all day. Just 30–60 minutes where no device is allowed to ping you. Leave your phone in another room, or turn on airplane mode.

2. **Pick one slow activity and do only that.**  
   Read a physical book. Write by hand. Take a walk with no audio. Practice an instrument. Sketch. The content matters less than the mode: one thing, extended time, no multitasking.

3. **Expect discomfort and boredom at first; don’t treat them as failure.**  
   When your mind protests—This is boring; I should check something—that’s not a reason to stop. That’s the training effect beginning.

4. **Stay with it long enough to cross the “threshold.”**  
   Often there’s a point, 10–20 minutes in, where the internal noise starts to recede and you sink into the activity. That shift is worth protecting. Notice how it feels different from your usual fragmented attention.

5. **Afterward, briefly reflect.**  
   Ask yourself: How did I feel before, during, and after? Was there anything I noticed that I normally wouldn’t? This doesn’t need to be a long journal, just a few honest sentences.

If you do this daily for a couple of weeks, you’re not going to suddenly transform your entire life. But you will probably experience a subtle recalibration. Things that felt impossible—reading for half an hour, concentrating on a problem, listening fully—start to feel more natural.

You’ll also get a clearer sense of what genuinely interests you versus what simply hooks you.

---

## A Different Kind of Interesting

The word “interesting” has had a strange evolution. In conversation, calling something “interesting” can be a way of politely dodging judgment. Online, it often just means “attention-grabbing.”

There is another, older sense of interesting: that which genuinely *engages your intellect or concern*, that which draws you deeper into reality instead of away from it.

The first kind of interesting is all about surface-level reaction. It’s the curiosity that says, “What’s next?” and never waits to hear the answer. The second kind is quieter and more durable. It says, “What’s actually going on here?” and is willing to stay.

Some things are low in the first sense (they don’t make for great posts, they’re not showy) but high in the second: a subtle scientific problem, a long-standing tension in a relationship, a difficult part of your own story, a craft that takes years to master.

If you unconsciously adopt the first definition as your primary guide, you’ll find yourself perpetually skimming across the surface, feeling informed but not grounded, stimulated but not fulfilled.

Choosing the second definition does not require rejecting technology, abandoning social media, or moving to a cabin. It mostly requires noticing what your habits do to your mind, and gently, persistently creating space for a different rhythm.

It means, at least sometimes, letting yourself be unoptimizable, unmonetized, unseen. Doing things nobody asked you to do. Thinking thoughts that don’t fit anywhere in a feed. Giving your full presence to moments that will never be recorded.

You probably won’t remember most of the things that held your attention this week. The clever posts, the small outrages, the minor news, the algorithmically perfect clips—they’ll blur together. But some quiet, unremarkable choices about what to attend to may, cumulatively, decide who you become.

There’s a sentence that’s been repeated in different forms by many thinkers: “You become what you pay attention to.” It’s poetic, but also, in a way, literal.

Attention is the lens that shapes what enters your mind, the fertilizer that makes some thoughts grow and others wither. Over time, the things you repeatedly attend to become your assumptions, your memories, your skills, your character.

In a world that is constantly trying to capture your attention, one of the most radical, gentle, and human acts left is simply this: to decide, for yourself, what is worth noticing.

## Friction as a Feature

Much of the design ethos around technology has been to remove friction. One-click buying, auto-play, default logins, push notifications: all meant to minimize the effort between wanting something and getting it.

For some tasks this is undeniably good—paying bills, accessing information quickly in emergencies, coordinating logistics. But when everything becomes effortless, we lose the natural pauses that used to serve as checkpoints for reflection.

Years ago, if you wanted a new book, you had to go to a store or library. The travel, the browsing, the walking home—all of that downtime gave your mind chances to reconsider: Do I really want this? What am I actually looking for?

Now, you can tap twice and have a book on your device in seconds. That’s convenient, but the decision is compressed to a momentary impulse, easily swayed by a polished description or a spike of curiosity that might vanish an hour later.

Friction, in moderation, protects you from your own whims.

There’s a strong case for intentionally reintroducing a little friction into your own attention economy:

- Deleting apps that are too easily opened; using them only in a browser.
- Turning off non-essential notifications entirely.
- Keeping certain activities (like reading or writing) tied to physical locations or objects.
- Adding small rituals before starting something important: a cup of tea, a walk, a few deep breaths.

These aren’t about becoming austere or Luddite. They’re about giving your reflective mind a fighting chance to step in before your reflexive habits steer everything.

When there’s a brief delay between impulse and action, you get a tiny window in which to ask: Is this really how I want to spend the next 30 minutes of my one life?

---

## The Value of Doing Things Badly

Another subtle effect of the attention economy is a distorted sense of competence. Online, you mostly encounter highly polished results: the best performances, the wittiest comments, the most aesthetic photos, the most articulate essays. The learning curves are hidden.

When you try something new and your first attempts are clumsy, it’s easy to feel outclassed, as if you’re miles behind some invisible standard. Combined with a feed that’s optimized to show you the most impressive examples, this can be paralyzing.

This is where the skill of *doing things badly* becomes crucial.

Doing something badly—not carelessly, but as a beginner—requires a quiet defiance. You’re saying, in effect:

“I am willing to be unimpressive for as long as it takes to build real competence.”

That stance is almost the opposite of what most algorithmic environments reward. Those environments favor *rapid micro-impressions*—a joke, a take, a highlight reel—that can be evaluated in seconds. Slow, uneven, incremental progression can’t compete in that space.

But away from the screen, in real human time, the willingness to be bad is the gatekeeper for growth. If you protect your image too carefully, if you’re unwilling to show up as a beginner, you shrink your life to only those arenas where you can already perform.

Some of the richest experiences you can have—learning an instrument, speaking a new language with real people, trying a new physical activity, making art, having vulnerable conversations—require a long period of being not-very-good. Online, that’s embarrassing. Offline, it’s just called “learning.”

If you can detach your sense of worth from immediate excellence, you free yourself to explore much more territory. You become less dependent on external validation and more guided by curiosity and inherent satisfaction.

---

## Attention as an Ethical Act

We usually talk about attention as a private matter: my focus, my habits, my productivity. But where you place your attention also has ethical dimensions, because attention is part of how value circulates in a culture.

Every time you click, share, watch, buy, or respond, you send a bit of signal into a complex network of systems. That signal—aggregated with millions of others—affects what gets made, who gets paid, what people think is acceptable, and what spreads.

This doesn’t mean your individual actions control the world. But they participate in shaping it.

- Giving attention to thoughtful, nuanced work—articles, books, conversations—supports an environment where nuance can survive.
- Feeding outrage cycles, even when you’re “hate-watching” or doomscrolling, strengthens the patterns that produce more of the same.
- Choosing to listen deeply to a friend instead of half-listening while scrolling sends a signal in the opposite direction: that depth and presence matter more than ambient distraction.

In this sense, attention functions a bit like money or votes. You don’t control the whole system, but you do participate. Small, repeated choices compound.

This doesn’t require becoming austere or moralistic about everything you consume. It does suggest, though, that you’re never entirely a passive observer. You are, in ways both subtle and direct, a contributor to the environment you inhabit.

Attending to something is not only a way of seeing; it’s also a way of saying, quietly: “Let there be more of this.”

---

## Reclaiming a Piece of Your Mind

If all of this sounds abstract, here’s a simple way to make it concrete. Suppose you wanted, very modestly, to reclaim some of your attention from the default currents around you. You might try something like this:

1. **Set aside one “notification-free” block per day.**  
   Not all day. Just 30–60 minutes where no device is allowed to ping you. Leave your phone in another room, or turn on airplane mode.

2. **Pick one slow activity and do only that.**  
   Read a physical book. Write by hand. Take a walk with no audio. Practice an instrument. Sketch. The content matters less than the mode: one thing, extended time, no multitasking.

3. **Expect discomfort and boredom at first; don’t treat them as failure.**  
   When your mind protests—This is boring; I should check something—that’s not a reason to stop. That’s the training effect beginning.

4. **Stay with it long enough to cross the “threshold.”**  
   Often there’s a point, 10–20 minutes in, where the internal noise starts to recede and you sink into the activity. That shift is worth protecting. Notice how it feels different from your usual fragmented attention.

5. **Afterward, briefly reflect.**  
   Ask yourself: How did I feel before, during, and after? Was there anything I noticed that I normally wouldn’t? This doesn’t need to be a long journal, just a few honest sentences.

If you do this daily for a couple of weeks, you’re not going to suddenly transform your entire life. But you will probably experience a subtle recalibration. Things that felt impossible—reading for half an hour, concentrating on a problem, listening fully—start to feel more natural.

You’ll also get a clearer sense of what genuinely interests you versus what simply hooks you.

---

## A Different Kind of Interesting

The word “interesting” has had a strange evolution. In conversation, calling something “interesting” can be a way of politely dodging judgment. Online, it often just means “attention-grabbing.”

There is another, older sense of interesting: that which genuinely *engages your intellect or concern*, that which draws you deeper into reality instead of away from it.

The first kind of interesting is all about surface-level reaction. It’s the curiosity that says, “What’s next?” and never waits to hear the answer. The second kind is quieter and more durable. It says, “What’s actually going on here?” and is willing to stay.

Some things are low in the first sense (they don’t make for great posts, they’re not showy) but high in the second: a subtle scientific problem, a long-standing tension in a relationship, a difficult part of your own story, a craft that takes years to master.

If you unconsciously adopt the first definition as your primary guide, you’ll find yourself perpetually skimming across the surface, feeling informed but not grounded, stimulated but not fulfilled.

Choosing the second definition does not require rejecting technology, abandoning social media, or moving to a cabin. It mostly requires noticing what your habits do to your mind, and gently, persistently creating space for a different rhythm.

It means, at least sometimes, letting yourself be unoptimizable, unmonetized, unseen. Doing things nobody asked you to do. Thinking thoughts that don’t fit anywhere in a feed. Giving your full presence to moments that will never be recorded.

You probably won’t remember most of the things that held your attention this week. The clever posts, the small outrages, the minor news, the algorithmically perfect clips—they’ll blur together. But some quiet, unremarkable choices about what to attend to may, cumulatively, decide who you become.

There’s a sentence that’s been repeated in different forms by many thinkers: “You become what you pay attention to.” It’s poetic, but also, in a way, literal.

Attention is the lens that shapes what enters your mind, the fertilizer that makes some thoughts grow and others wither. Over time, the things you repeatedly attend to become your assumptions, your memories, your skills, your character.

In a world that is constantly trying to capture your attention, one of the most radical, gentle, and human acts left is simply this: to decide, for yourself, what is worth noticing.
